title: Psalms 37
subtitle: Do Not Fret Because of the Wicked

sections.header: Verses 1–20 — “Trust in the LORD and Do Good”

heb.paragraph: "Trust (bāṭaḥ) in the LORD and do good; dwell in the land and enjoy safe pasture" (v.3). An acrostic wisdom psalm. David counsels: do not fret about the wicked (v.1), trust God (v.3), delight in the LORD and he will give you the desires of your heart (v.4), commit your way to him (v.5), be still and wait (v.7). "The meek will inherit the land" (v.11) — Jesus quotes this in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:5).

refs.note: Proverbs 24 shares the psalm's opening injunction almost verbatim: 'Do not fret because of evildoers … for the evildoer has no future hope.' Wisdom and worship traditions converge on the same counsel.
Jesus' beatitude 'Blessed are the meek, for they will inherit the earth' quotes Psalm 37:11 directly. The psalm's promise to the ʿănāwîm becomes the charter of the kingdom of heaven.
Hebrews' call to patient endurance — 'in just a little while, he who is coming will come and will not delay' — draws on the psalm's repeated counsel to wait patiently for the LORD (vv. 7, 9, 34).

notes.note: MacArthur: the promise is conditional — 'Delight in the Lord' comes before 'he will give you the desires of your heart.' When believers genuinely delight in God, their desires conform to his will. The promise is not wish fulfilment but sanctified desire.

MacArthur: Jesus elevated this verse to a Beatitude (Matt 5:5), confirming its enduring significance. The meek are not weak but submitted to God's sovereign plan, trusting his timing rather than seizing control themselves.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the psalm opens with the acrostic's constraint harnessing explosive emotion. The content screams injustice; the form demands patience. The tension between rage at wickedness and the discipline of alphabetic order enacts the psalm's own counsel: do not fret.

Alter: 'the meek will inherit the land' — 'anāwîm are not the timid but the afflicted who wait on God. Jesus quotes this in Matthew 5:5. The Hebrew 'ereṣ (land) becomes in the Beatitudes the whole earth.

Calvin: 'Do not fret' — the Hebrew (ḥārah) means 'burn.' Calvin: envy is a slow burn that consumes the envious, not the envied. The command is both moral (do not sin) and practical (do not destroy yourself).

Calvin: 'Delight in the Lord, and he will give you the desires of your heart' — Calvin warns against reading this as a blank cheque. Delighting in God reshapes desire itself. The promise is not 'God gives whatever you want' but 'God becomes what you want.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: this is an acrostic psalm — each pair of verses begins with a successive Hebrew letter. The wisdom-psalm genre combines proverbial instruction with psalmist trust language, creating a teaching hymn.

NET: 'inherit the land' — the verb yāraš and noun 'ereṣ echo the Deuteronomic land promise (Deut 4:1, 16:20). The righteous poor will receive what God promised Israel: secure possession of the promised land.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: six imperatives in seven verses — trust, do good, delight, commit, be still, wait. The cure for anxiety about the wicked is not analysis of their behaviour but redirection of one's own attention toward God.
Kidner: 'Better the little that the righteous have' — the 'better than' proverb format (cf. Prov 15:16-17) relativises wealth. Value is measured not by quantity but by whose hands hold it.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: 'delight in the Lord' — the Hebrew 'ānag means to take exquisite pleasure. The relationship with God is not duty but delight. When God becomes the supreme pleasure, lesser desires realign themselves.

VanGemeren: Jesus' quotation of this verse in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:5) globalises the promise. The 'land' expands from Canaan to the whole earth; the 'meek' expands from Israel to all who trust God's timing.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the grass metaphor is deliberately mundane. The wicked are not compared to cosmic forces or mighty beasts but to grass — common, forgettable, gone by afternoon. Their threat is overrated.
Goldingay: 'Be still' (dōm) means literally 'be silent.' The counsel is not merely emotional calm but verbal restraint — stop arguing the case, stop protesting the injustice, and let God be judge.

hist.context: Psalm 37 is an acrostic wisdom psalm that addresses the perennial problem of the wicked prospering while the righteous suffer — the same question that drives Job and Ecclesiastes. The historical context likely reflects a period when Israelite society was stratified enough for the gap between righteous poor and wicked rich to be visible and distressing. The psalm's advice to 'not fret' (vv.1, 7, 8) responds to a genuine temptation: watching unjust prosperity can erode faith in God's moral governance. The agricultural metaphors ('they will fade like green pasture,' v.2) suggest a rural audience familiar with seasonal cycles of growth and withering.

sections.header: Verses 21–40 — “I Have Never Seen the Righteous Forsaken”

heb.paragraph: "I was young and now I am old, yet I have never seen the righteous (ṣaddîq) forsaken or their children begging bread" (v.25). David’s lifetime testimony. The righteous are sustained through all seasons. The wicked flourish briefly and vanish like smoke (v.20). "The salvation of the righteous comes from the LORD; he is their stronghold in time of trouble" (v.39).

refs.note: Psalm 73 wrestles with the same problem — the prosperity of the wicked — but from an experiential rather than instructional angle. Together, Psalms 37 and 73 form the most sustained biblical reflection on theodicy.

Peter's assurance that 'the Lord is not slow in keeping his promise' addresses the same tension: apparent divine inaction in the face of evil. The psalm's counsel to wait patiently finds its eschatological answer in Peter.

notes.note: MacArthur: God directs and sustains the believer's path. Stumbling is possible, but permanent falling is prevented by divine support. This is the doctrine of perseverance — God's power keeps his people secure.

MacArthur: patient waiting for the Lord is one of the most difficult spiritual disciplines. This psalm repeatedly counsels it (vv.7, 9, 34) because it is the practical expression of trust in God's sovereign timing.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'I was young and now I am old' — the testimony of a lifetime. Alter notes this is experiential wisdom, not dogma. The aged psalmist reports what he has observed, knowing others may observe differently.

Alter: the wicked man 'flourishing like a luxuriant native tree' who then vanishes — the botanical image is the psalm's most vivid. The tree was rooted, tall, and green. Then it was simply gone. Permanence was illusion.

Calvin: 'though he may stumble, he will not fall' — Calvin distinguishes stumbling (temporary failure) from falling (permanent ruin). The righteous person is not guaranteed a life without misstep, but divine hands prevent the misstep from becoming catastrophe.
Calvin: 'Hope in the Lord and keep his way' — the two commands belong together. Hope without obedience is presumption; obedience without hope is legalism. Calvin holds them in dynamic tension.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: this verse is experiential testimony, not absolute promise. It reflects the psalmist's personal observation across a long life. Other psalms (e.g., 73) wrestle with cases where the righteous do suffer deprivation.

NET: 'like a luxuriant native tree' — the Hebrew is difficult. The MT reads 'like a native green tree' (ke'ezrāḥ ra'anān). The point is deep rootedness — the wicked appeared permanently established but were not.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the testimony 'I have never seen the righteous forsaken' is personal, not universal. Kidner notes it honestly: this is one man's witness, not a systematic theology. Job and the cross both qualify it.

Kidner: the vanishing tree is the psalm's master illustration. It was there — then it was not. No gradual decline, no transition. One day flourishing, the next day gone. The wicked's permanence is always illusion.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: 'the Lord loves the just' — 'āhav (love) combined with mišpāṭ (justice) reveals the covenant God's character. His love is not indifferent to moral reality; his justice is animated by love.

VanGemeren: the psalm's conclusion is summary theology: salvation, stronghold, help, deliverance — four rescue words. The righteous take refuge; God delivers. The relationship is responsive, covenantal, and certain.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: 'the law of their God is in their hearts' — this internalisation of Torah anticipates Jeremiah 31:33. The righteous do not merely obey externally; wisdom has become instinct. Their feet do not slip because Torah is their balance.

Goldingay: the tree metaphor is the psalm's emotional climax. The Hebrew 'I passed by' (wā'e'ebōr) is devastatingly casual. The wicked's disappearance is so complete that even looking for them yields nothing.

hist.context: The land-promise theology of Psalm 37:21-40 — 'the meek will inherit the land' (v.11, cited by Jesus in Matthew 5:5) — connects the wisdom tradition to the Deuteronomic covenant. The repeated assurance that the righteous will 'dwell in the land' (vv.3, 9, 11, 22, 29, 34) echoes the Abrahamic and Mosaic promises, grounding personal ethics in national covenant theology. The psalmist's autobiographical testimony ('I was young and now I am old,' v.25) claims the authority of a lifetime of observation — this is empirical wisdom confirmed by personal experience over decades.

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: Aged wisdom teacher addressing the problem of evil (vv.25,35-36)
ppl.text: David writes as an old man looking back: 'I was young and now I am old, yet I have never seen the righteous forsaken or their children begging bread' (v.25). This acrostic psalm is wisdom literature in poetic form, structured as a series of imperatives: 'trust,' 'delight,' 'commit,' 'be still' (vv.3-7). Rather than explaining evil philosophically, David prescribes a posture — patient trust that God's justice operates on a longer timeline than human impatience allows.

ppl.name: God (Yahweh)
ppl.role: Righteous judge who will settle accounts in his timing (vv.9-11,28-29,33-34)
ppl.text: Yahweh 'laughs at the wicked, for he knows their day is coming' (v.13) — divine laughter expressing confident sovereignty, not cruelty. The meek 'will inherit the land' (v.11), a promise Jesus cites in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:5), expanding 'land' from Canaan to the renewed earth. God 'upholds the righteous' (v.17) and 'will not forsake his faithful ones' (v.28) — covenant guarantees anchored in character, not circumstance.

ppl.name: The Wicked vs. The Righteous
ppl.text: The psalm sets up a sustained contrast: the wicked 'borrow and do not repay' while the righteous 'give generously' (v.21); the wicked 'plot against the righteous' (v.12) but their own sword 'will pierce their own hearts' (v.15). This is not naive optimism but a faith conviction: apparent prosperity of the wicked is temporary ('like the beauty of the fields they will vanish,' v.20), while the righteous have an eternal inheritance.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVfor like the grass they will soon wither, like green plants they will soon die away.ESVFor they will soon fade like the grass and wither like the green herb.NIVBe still before the Lord and wait patiently for him; do not fret when people succeed in their ways, when they carry out their wicked schemes.ESVBe still before the LORD and wait patiently for him; fret not yourself over the one who prospers in his way, over the man who carries out evil devices!

rows.label: A Lifetime’s Testimony — 37:21–40
rows.text: "I was young and now I am old — never seen the righteous forsaken." Stronghold in trouble.

hebtext.note: "Trust (bāṭaḥ) in the LORD and do good; dwell in the land and enjoy safe pasture" (v.3). An acrostic wisdom psalm. David counsels: do not fret about the wicked (v.1), trust God (v.3), delight in the LORD and he will give you the desires of your heart (v.4), commit your way to him (v.5), be still and wait (v.7). "The meek will inherit the land" (v.11) — Jesus quotes this in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:5).

"I was young and now I am old, yet I have never seen the righteous (ṣaddîq) forsaken or their children begging bread" (v.25). David’s lifetime testimony. The righteous are sustained through all seasons. The wicked flourish briefly and vanish like smoke (v.20). "The salvation of the righteous comes from the LORD; he is their stronghold in time of trouble" (v.39).

themes.note: Faith peaks: trust, delight, commit, be still — the four verbs of Psalm 37 faith. Prophecy: "the meek inherit the land" quoted by Jesus (Matt 5:5). Justice: the wicked fade; the righteous endure.

prayer_prompt: As you consider 'Do not fret because of the wicked,' consider asking God whether anxiety about other people's success or injustice is consuming energy He wants you to spend on faithfulness instead.
